title: Watermarks on the mould
subtitle: Attaching wire designs to a papermaking mould to form watermarks in every sheet
excerpt: A watermark is formed not in the paper but in the mould: fine wire bent into a design and stitched to the mould surface creates a slightly raised area where the pulp lies thinner. In the finished sheet, held to light, the design appears as a brighter, more translucent zone. This tutorial covers bending, attaching, and using a simple wire watermark.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1947), chapter on watermarks. Hunter documents the wire construction and attachment method used in historical European paper mills. Internet Archive. Albert Haemmerle, Buntpapier (1961), translated extracts on watermark wire techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: A translucent design visible in a sheet of paper when held to light, formed by a wire pattern on the mould surface that causes pulp to settle thinner in that area.
The widely spaced parallel lines visible in laid paper when held to light, formed by heavier wires that run across the width of the mould at regular intervals.
The closely spaced parallel lines that run the length of a laid paper mould. Together with chain lines they form the characteristic grid pattern of laid paper.

content.text: is built by bending fine wire into a shape and stitching it to the surface of the mould with even finer wire. When the sheet forms over it, the fibres rest on the raised wire and settle thinner there. The technique requires patience in the bending and stitching stage but no special equipment beyond wire, pliers, and a needle. The
already on the mould appear as background texture in the finished sheet; the attached watermark design reads as a separate, brighter shape against that texture.

items.name: Laid or wove papermaking mould (an old or spare mould — the wire stitching is permanent)
Fine brass or phosphor-bronze wire, 0.3 to 0.5 mm diameter (for the design outline)
Very fine wire, 0.1 to 0.15 mm diameter (for stitching the design to the mould)
Large sewing needle with a large eye (for threading the stitching wire)
Pencil and paper (for sketching the design at full size before bending)

content.text: Keep the first design simple: a monogram, a circle, or a leaf outline. The wire must be bent to exactly the right shape because it cannot be adjusted once stitched. Draw the design full-size on paper, then bend the wire to match the drawing, checking the wire against the paper template at each stage. Closed shapes (circles, ovals) need a small overlap at the join, which is then stitched down flat against the mould surface.
Stitching the design to the mould
Place the bent wire design on the mould face-up in the intended position. Mark the position lightly with a pencil dot at each corner of the design.

Thread a length of fine stitching wire through the needle. Starting at one end of the design wire, pass the stitching wire under one laid line of the mould, up over the design wire, and back under the next laid line. This creates a small saddle stitch that holds the design wire against the mould. Repeat every 3 to 4 mm along the design.

At curves, place stitches closer together (every 2 mm) to keep the curve from lifting. At corners, stitch on both sides of the corner point. Pull each stitch snug but do not distort the mould wire surface.
Finish by threading the stitching wire under several existing stitches and trimming flush. No knots: the wire tail simply tucks under the previous stitches and friction holds it.
Forming sheets over a watermarked mould

Use the mould exactly as described in the cotton linter or abaca sheet-forming tutorials. The watermark will appear in every sheet formed. For the clearest watermark, use a slightly thinner vat concentration than usual and form sheets slowly. Hold each finished sheet to a window before drying to check whether the watermark reads clearly.

attrs.heading: Common watermark problems
attrs.intro: Problems with watermark visibility and wire stability.

items.symptom: Watermark is invisible or very faint in the finished sheet
items.cause: Design wire too thin relative to the mould surface, or vat concentration too heavy.
items.fix: Use 0.5 mm wire for the design outline. Reduce pulp concentration in the vat.

items.symptom: Design wire lifts from the mould surface during sheet forming
items.cause: Stitching too widely spaced, or stitching wire pulled too loosely.
items.fix: Add more stitching saddles at 2 mm intervals. Re-stitch any lifted sections before forming more sheets.
